subject: Chemistry
title: Radium Paint: Glowing Watches of Death
description: A fast breakdown of Radium Paint: Glowing Watches of Death for builders.

hook.text: In the 1920s, women painted watch dials with radium to make them glow. They licked their brushes to sharpen the tips.

buildup.text: Radium mixed with zinc sulfide creates a self-glowing paint. The radiation excites the zinc sulfide, which emits light.

discovery.text: The 'Radium Girls' ingested radium daily. It deposited in their bones, destroying jaw tissue and causing cancer.

twist.text: Their employer knew radium was dangerous and denied it. The Radium Girls' lawsuit created modern worker safety laws.

climax.text: Their bones still emit radiation nearly 100 years later. Radium-226 has a half-life of 1,600 years.

punchline.text: Glowing paint, licked brushes, radioactive bones. Beauty that outlasts the body.

quiz.question: What lasting impact did the Radium Girls case have?
options: Their lawsuit helped establish modern workplace safety laws
It led to the discovery of radium's radioactive properties
It banned all luminous paints from consumer products
